answer:
Definition

Dark Mode provides an alternate color scheme with dark backgrounds and light text, reducing eye strain in low-light environments and saving battery on OLED screens. In production apps, it signals design maturity and user-centricity.

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub (React + Tailwind + shadcn/ui), dark mode is implemented via Tailwind's darkMode: 'class' strategy. A toggle in the navbar adds/removes the dark class on <html>, persisted to localStorage. All shadcn/ui components use CSS variables (--background, --foreground) that swap automatically.

Code Example

// ThemeToggle.tsx
import { useEffect, useState } from 'react';

export function ThemeToggle() {
  const [dark, setDark] = useState(() =>
    localStorage.getItem('theme') === 'dark'
  );

  useEffect(() => {
    document.documentElement.classList.toggle('dark', dark);
    localStorage.setItem('theme', dark ? 'dark' : 'light');
  }, [dark]);

  return (
    
  );
}

/* globals.css */
:root {
  --background: 0 0% 100%;
  --foreground: 222 47% 11%;
}
.dark {
  --background: 222 47% 11%;
  --foreground: 210 40% 98%;
}

Debugging Story

In ContactsHub, dark mode worked on the contacts list but the image-crop modal (Canvas API) had a white background that blinded users. The modal used a hard-coded background: white instead of the CSS variable. Manoj replaced it with bg-background (Tailwind class mapped to the CSS variable), fixing the inconsistency.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Flash of wrong theme on page load — the page renders light before JS toggles the dark class.
Mitigation: Add a blocking <script> in <head> that reads localStorage and sets the class before the page renders.

Validation Strategy

  • Playwright E2E: toggle theme → screenshot → assert background color changes.
  • Axe contrast audit in both light and dark modes.
  • Visual regression test for every page in both themes.

Definition

The top 3 Dark Mode mistakes are: not using CSS variables for colors, forgetting images/SVGs that assume light backgrounds, and the flash-of-unstyled-theme (FOUT) on page load.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the Recharts-based leave analytics chart used hard-coded colors (#1e40af for bars). In dark mode, the blue bars were invisible against the dark background. Manoj created a theme-aware color map that reads CSS variables, so chart colors adapt to the active theme.

Code Example

// ❌ BAD: Hard-coded chart colors


// ✅ GOOD: Theme-aware chart colors
const chartColor = getComputedStyle(document.documentElement)
  .getPropertyValue('--chart-primary').trim();


/* globals.css */
:root { --chart-primary: #3b82f6; }
.dark { --chart-primary: #93c5fd; }

Debugging Story

In the HR system, the company logo (a dark SVG) disappeared in dark mode. Manoj solved it by creating two logo variants — dark and light — and conditionally rendering based on the dark class: <img src={isDark ? '/logo-light.svg' : '/logo-dark.svg'} />.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Third-party widgets (e.g., date pickers, rich text editors) that don't respect your CSS variable theme, staying permanently light.
Mitigation: Wrap third-party widgets in a themed container and use CSS specificity overrides to inject your dark-mode variables.

Validation Strategy

  • Automated Playwright test: toggle dark mode → screenshot every page → visual diff.
  • Axe contrast audit in dark mode (separate CI step from light mode).
  • Manual QA checklist for charts, modals, and third-party widgets in both themes.

Definition

Animation Performance ensures CSS and JS animations run at 60fps by using GPU-accelerated properties (transform, opacity) and avoiding layout-triggering properties (width, height, top, left). Jank-free animations make the app feel professional and responsive.

Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the contact-card hover animation originally used box-shadow transition, which triggered paint on every frame. Manoj switched to transform: translateY(-2px) + opacity change for the shadow layer, moving the animation to the compositor thread — eliminating jank on the 500-contact list page.

Code Example

/* ❌ SLOW: triggers layout + paint */
.card:hover {
  box-shadow: 0 8px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);
  margin-top: -2px;
}

/* ✅ FAST: compositor-only properties */
.card {
  will-change: transform;
  transition: transform 200ms ease, opacity 200ms ease;
}
.card:hover {
  transform: translateY(-2px);
}

/* Shadow via pseudo-element with opacity */
.card::after {
  content: '';
  position: absolute;
  inset: 0;
  border-radius: inherit;
  box-shadow: 0 8px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);
  opacity: 0;
  transition: opacity 200ms ease;
}
.card:hover::after {
  opacity: 1;
}

Debugging Story

In ContactsHub, the contact-list page was janky when scrolling with hover animations active. Chrome DevTools Performance tab showed green paint rectangles on every card hover. By isolating the shadow to a pseudo-element with opacity transition and adding will-change: transform, paint was eliminated — only composite operations remained.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Overusing will-change on every element, which consumes GPU memory and can actually worsen performance.
Mitigation: Only apply will-change to elements that actively animate. Remove it when the animation completes for one-time transitions.

Validation Strategy

  • Chrome DevTools Performance tab: record scroll + hover → assert no layout/paint events during animation.
  • Lighthouse performance score ≥ 90 with animations enabled.
  • FPS counter during Playwright scroll test — assert ≥ 55fps average.

Definition

The top 3 Animation Performance mistakes are: animating layout-triggering properties, overusing will-change, and running complex JS animations on the main thread instead of using CSS transitions or the Web Animations API.

Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Dashboard, the KPI counter animation used setInterval to increment numbers from 0 to the target value, running on the main thread and blocking input. Manoj replaced it with requestAnimationFrame and added will-change: contents only during the animation, then removed it after completion.

Code Example

// ❌ BAD: setInterval counter
setInterval(() => {
  el.textContent = ++count;
}, 16); // blocks main thread

// ✅ GOOD: requestAnimationFrame counter
function animateCounter(el, target, duration = 1000) {
  const start = performance.now();
  el.style.willChange = 'contents';

  function step(now) {
    const progress = Math.min((now - start) / duration, 1);
    el.textContent = Math.round(progress * target);
    if (progress < 1) requestAnimationFrame(step);
    else el.style.willChange = 'auto'; // cleanup
  }
  requestAnimationFrame(step);
}

Debugging Story

In the CRM Dashboard, the animated pie chart for lead distribution froze the page for 200ms on load. The chart library was animating SVG d attribute changes via JS. Manoj switched to CSS stroke-dashoffset animation on SVG circles, which runs on the compositor thread — the chart now animates at 60fps with zero main-thread blocking.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Using CSS transition: all 0.3s which transitions every property change, including layout properties you didn't intend to animate.
Mitigation: Always specify exact properties: transition: transform 0.3s, opacity 0.3s.

Validation Strategy

  • Chrome DevTools Performance: record page load → assert no long tasks during animation.
  • CI Lighthouse check: Total Blocking Time < 200ms.
  • Playwright test: measure frame rate during KPI counter animation using performance.now() deltas.

Definition

The top 3 Design System mistakes are: building too many components too early, not documenting component APIs, and creating rigid components that can't be composed together.

Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In Manoj's CRM Dashboard, the team initially built 30+ custom components in the first sprint — most were never used. Manoj refactored to a minimal set: Button, Card, Input, Select, Table, Modal, and Badge. These 7 components covered 95% of the UI. New components were added only when needed by a second feature.

Code Example

// ❌ BAD: Rigid component with too many built-in features
function Card({ title, subtitle, image, actions, footer, badge }) {
  // 6 props, most optional, complex internal logic
}

// ✅ GOOD: Composable compound component
function Card({ children, className }) {
  return 
{children}
;
}
Card.Header = ({ children }) => 
{children}
;
Card.Body = ({ children }) => 
{children}
;
Card.Footer = ({ children }) => 
{children}
;

// Usage

  Lead: Acme Corp
  $50,000 pipeline value
  Qualified

Debugging Story

In the CRM Dashboard, a DataCard component had 12 props for every possible variation. When a new requirement needed a card with a chart inside, none of the props supported it. Manoj refactored to a compound Card pattern — Card.Header, Card.Body, Card.Footer — making any content composable inside the card shell.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Creating a design system in isolation without consuming it in real features — the API drifts from actual developer needs.
Mitigation: Build design system components alongside feature work. Extract to the system only after 2+ features use the same pattern.

Validation Strategy

  • Storybook with controls for every component prop — interactive documentation.
  • Import analysis: script that flags design system components imported in < 2 feature modules.
  • Visual regression test for every component variant in Storybook.

Definition

A Design System is a collection of reusable components, design tokens, and guidelines that ensure visual and behavioral consistency across an application. It's the single source of truth for UI — when everyone uses the same Button, Card, and Input, the app looks and feels cohesive.

Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the design system includes Angular Material components customized with VisionQuest's theme, plus custom components like ApprovalChainStepper, LeaveBalanceCard, and NotificationBanner. New features (like a time-off calendar) reuse these components — consistent look with zero design rework.

Code Example

// Design system export — barrel file
// src/components/ui/index.ts
export { Button } from './button';
export { Card } from './card';
export { Input } from './input';
export { Badge } from './badge';
export { Dialog } from './dialog';
export { DataTable } from './data-table';

// Feature uses design system
import { Card, Badge, Button } from '@/components/ui';

function LeaveRequestCard({ request }) {
  return (
    
      {request.type}
      
        {request.status}
        
{request.dates}

      
      
        
      
    
  );
}

Debugging Story

In the HR system, two developers built separate "status badge" components — one in the leave module, one in the notifications module. They had different colors for "approved" status. Manoj extracted a single StatusBadge into the design system with a canonical color map, eliminating the inconsistency and reducing duplicate code.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Building a design system without component documentation — developers can't discover what exists and build duplicates.
Mitigation: Use Storybook as living documentation. Every component gets a story with all variants and a props table.

Validation Strategy

  • Storybook Chromatic for visual regression on every PR.
  • Import audit: CI script ensures feature modules import from @/components/ui, not from local copies.
  • Quarterly design review: compare Figma designs against Storybook to catch drift.
